data.title: Sendas Distribuidora: Not Done From Deleveraging Gains
data.description:
Currently Brazil's second-largest supermarket chain, Sendas Distribuidora (OTC:ASAIY), popularly known as Assaí Atacadista, has been a major beneficiary of a very favorable scenario for cash-and-carry (C&C), reaping the rewards of rapid store expansion across the country over the last decade.

Initially, Assaí's expansion plan, financed by a high debt load, ended up putting pressure on the company's results due to the large number of stores in the process of maturing and the disbursements necessary to support the process (in a highly volatile interest rate environment in Brazil). In fact, the very tight correlation between Assaí's results and interest rate fluctuations in Brazil (Selic) was one of the main points of attention in the thesis I highlighted in my last coverage almost two years ago.

Over the past five years, Assaí's performance has been poor, reaching the peak of skepticism at the end of last year, with interest rates in Brazil reaching 15%, putting high pressure on the company's leverage. This year, however, signs of stabilization in the Selic rate have already corrected the ADR price significantly, jumping more than 100% year-to-date.

But I don't believe that ASAIY's rebound has stopped there. As stores mature, the company is reducing its CapEx, exercising excellent cost control, and generating increasingly better free cash flow, paving the way for a beautiful deleveraging process with a concrete target for 2025.

Estimating that it is quite feasible for Assaí to continue reaping successes on the path to deleveraging in the next two quarters, and with growth estimates pointing to solid numbers this year and in the following years, I believe that Buy here is still warranted.

On ASAIY's Rate-Sensitivity

Quite simply (and somewhat obviously for C&C companies), Assaí behaves like an extremely rate-sensitive Brazilian consumer stock.

The ADR price tends to move inversely to tight domestic monetary policy. Therefore, the higher the Selic rate, the greater the pressure on ASAIY. Conversely, rate cuts or support tend to boost the ADR's price performance.

As can be seen in the chart below, Assaí's behavior (blue line) has typically been inversely related to Brazil's interest rate (pink line) in recent years, confirmed in the lower line with a negative correlation through fluctuations below zero in periods of tightening.

So, as a rule of thumb, this negative correlation that occurs in periods of Selic under pressure can serve as an early signal for entry positions.

Bringing it to the present context, at the moment, the interest rate (Selic) in Brazil stands at 15%, maintained since July, after a high of 10.5% since July last year.

As we can see in the chart below, post-pandemic inflation, which exceeded 10%, was controlled by massive rate hikes from 2% in March 2021 to 13.8% in September 2022, taking effect from that period and reaching the inflation target tolerance range (3% ±1.5%) a year later, in September 2023 (as shown by the blue arrow)—which served as support for rates to fall to 10.5% in mid-2024.

The problem is that, as shown by the red arrow above, contrary to expectations that inflation would remain within the tolerance range with a Selic rate of 10.5%, inflation rose throughout 2024, until in H2 it exceeded the tolerance range again for the first time since Sep/23. The result was a more hawkish Brazilian Central Bank, and rates were raised from 10.8% at the end of 2025 to 15% by then.

As projections indicate that, with stable rates, inflation may converge to the tolerance range starting in February 2026, with inflation expected to reach 4.39% per year, the market has been pricing in a possible scenario of rate cuts since the beginning of this year, or at least that the peak of monetary tightening has already passed.

Therefore, assets such as ASAYI, which are extremely rate-sensitive, have seen their performance jump sharply this year, largely above other Brazilian equities represented by EWZ's ETF (EWZ).

On ASAIY's Strategy and Leverage

The main reason for the extreme sensitivity of Assaí's thesis to interest rates is precisely because debt is one of the central points of the company's thesis—although not necessarily the only one.

Much of Assaí's aggressive growth over the past decade (gross revenue CAGR of 24%) has been financed by debt, with total debt increasing by 54% since 2020.

Assaí's "growth formula" combined expansion (new stores/volume) with financing via the debt/debenture market, which works as long as cash generation and margins support debt servicing.

So much so that today, Assaí has a 6-8% share of food retail in Brazil within a 20-25% C&C market, consolidating its dominance, especially in the C&C market in Brazil.

And while over the last five years, thanks to an increase in debt, revenues have expanded by 74.6%, EBITDA generation has grown by only 10% over the same period, leaving Assaí a highly leveraged company.

In 1Q23, for example, debt/EBITDA reached 4.7x, while currently, in 2Q25 (the most recent quarter), the company has 3.17x, aiming for a 2.6x in 4Q25.

This deleveraging target of 2.6x in the next two quarters is based on the following points:

  • Lower CapEx compared to previous years, with Assaí managing to dedicate 90% of EBITDA generation to debt reduction;
  • Discipline in working capital and inventory to free up cash;
  • Focus on controlling expenses, with SG&A virtually stabilized over the last decade at least;
  • Occasional prepayment of debt and potential refinancing have helped to optimize interest costs and accelerate leverage reduction.

Note how the evolution of Assaí's stores over the last decade, as a result of intense CapEx, has positively converged in EBITDA margin gains from 4.1% in 2011 to 7.6% in the last twelve months, which is extremely relevant in a super competitive and complex food retail segment in Brazil.

On Valuations

Based on the market's consensus revenue estimate of $15.04 billion (R$79.71 billion), and the EBITDA margin remaining at the same level of 7.6% (EBITDA of R$6.05 billion), and assuming a forward P/S of 0.17x, if Assaí manages to reach its deleveraging target for Fiscal 2025 to 2.6x (vs. 3.17x at the moment), this would imply a reduction in net debt from R$13.80 billion to ~R$11.3 billion.

If a constant EV is maintained, this would, in theory, raise the implicit market value of the ADR from $2.56 billion to $3.2 billion—an upside here of ~25%.

Of course, this figure depends heavily on the maintenance of the EV, as well as the use of real net debt and the EBITDA adopted. However, I believe that, based on a stable/falling interest rate scenario in Brazil, the cost of capital will tend to favor Assaí, which I estimate to be no lower than 12% today.

Risks and Final Remarks

Sendas Distribuidora, or rather Assaí Atacadista, triple-digit gain over the course of this year, does not mark the end of the upside line for the company.

Simply from an ongoing deleverage process that seems to be well consolidated to be fulfilled with feasible targets for this year would put a relevant upside to the thesis that I still don't believe the market has priced in accordingly.

A further macro recovery for Brazilian equities, supported by maintaining/falling interest rates, mainly supported by inflation coming in lower and within the Central Bank of Brazil's target, due to FX tailwinds (a weaker USD vs. BRL), contributes to an optimistic outlook for food retail companies.

It's important to note, however, that the Brazilian economy is volatile, and a reversal in the yield curve could drastically put Assaí's deleverage plan in a bad light, causing an unbridled sell-off of the ADR in a matter of days.

In particular, I believe that this macro scenario, although plausible, does not configure the current reality, and I am much more inclined towards an ongoing recovery on Brazilian equities, as in the case of Assaí, than the other way around.

That said, the confluence of a favorable macro plus Assaí's very organized and stable operating momentum converging into a deleveraging progress makes it a Buy rating desirable in my view, at least for the short-term (~6 months range).

data.title: Kinder Morgan: A Natural Gas Gem With A 4% Yield
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) is a rapidly-growing midstream platform with considerable distributable cash flow and earnings strength. The energy enterprise is growing quickly in its core natural gas business and it has added billions of dollars to its project backlog in recent years… especially in highly promising regions such as the Permian Basin. I like that Kinder Morgan is doubling down on its natural gas business, which by far makes the biggest contribution to the midstream platform’s earnings before interest and depreciation. The firm's shares also remain attractively valued and have revaluation potential in the context of growing energy demand in the U.S. The dividend is well-supported by estimated DCF and the energy enterprise should have no problems growing its dividend next year.

Previous rating

I previously rated shares of Kinder Morgan a buy -- A Top Natural Gas Bet For Growth And Yield (Rating Upgrade) -- due to the midstream platform 1) Growing its distributable cash flow, 2) Having very decent dividend coverage, and 3) Likely benefiting from incremental energy demand driven by Data Centers. While the energy enterprise in the past went overboard with debt-financed acquisitions -- which resulted in a dividend cut in 2016 -- I like that the midstream company now has very strong dividend coverage. The growth backlog indicates that Kinder Morgan has a filled pipeline with projects that have the potential to make incremental DCF contributions in the future.

Natural gas-heavy footprint in key operational areas

Kinder Morgan is one of the largest midstream businesses and pipeline operators in North America. With a market cap of $61.8B, Kinder Morgan competes against other behemoths in the midstream industry such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ENB) and MPLX (MPLX).

Kinder Morgan owns an extensive pipeline network that it uses to transport energy raw materials, such as natural gas and refined products, to consumer end-markets. The pipeline operator owns ~66,000 miles of natural gas pipelines and has other complementary assets as well, including refined products pipelines, terminals and storage facilities. The midstream platform has made an effort especially to grow its footprint in the high-potential Permian Basin, which connects producers to both end markets as well as export facilities along the U.S. Gulf Coast.

Natural gas pipelines is the largest segment for Kinder Morgan and it contributed $1,347M to the midstream platform's adjusted earnings before depreciation and amortization in the second quarter, representing 65% of all segment EBDA and showing 10% year-over-year growth. The firm's total EBDA increased 6% year-over-year to $2,081M in Q2'25 and the natural gas business is by far the one with the fastest growth.

While there are midstream companies that are larger than Kinder Morgan, the midstream platform is nonetheless a heavyweight in the North American energy market. Kinder Morgan transports approximately 40% of all domestic natural gas production to consumer end markets, which makes the energy enterprise an indispensable pillar of the U.S. energy market.

Demand for U.S. natural gas is expected to grow rapidly in the years ahead as the industrial base grows and relatively new parts of the U.S. economy -- such as the Data Center sector -- are expected to be major catalysts for energy demand growth. In fact, business consulting firm McKinsey is projecting that U.S. Data Centers will more than double their share of total U.S. power between 2025 until 2030. Earlier work from McKinsey also stated that the U.S. Data Center demand was set to expand at a 10% pace annually through 2030, highlighting sustained growth tailwinds for the firm's natural gas business.

As of the end of the second-quarter, Kinder Morgan's project backlog totaled $9.3B, showing a $1.2B increase since the end of FY 2024 and a $7.9B increase compared to FY 2021 levels. It is mainly the firm’s natural gas projects (92%) that are responsible for the addition of incremental backlog. With natural gas demand expected to grow consistently going forward, I believe Kinder Morgan has considerable development potential in this business.

These natural gas projects tend to be mainly expansions of already existing and operational assets. Most of these pipeline expansions will go in-service between FY 2026 and FY 2030, creating a long-term growth driver for Kinder Morgan's natural gas EBDA as well as distributable cash flow.

Kinder Morgan’s valuation

The midstream platform is currently valued at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 11.4X, which makes it the second-most expensive midstream play after Canadian energy champion Enbridge (ENB), whose shares are trading at an EBITDA multiplier of 13.0X. Enbridge also has a strong capital framework and is growing through acquisitions, which has resulted in me rating the energy enterprise a strong buy.

Enterprise Products Partners -- my all-time favorite MLP given the firm’s impressive 27-year distribution growth history -- is trading at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA of 10.0X while MPLX is priced at a 10.2X EV-to-EBITDA ratio. The industry group average here is 11.2X.

Kinder Morgan is therefore trading slightly above the industry group average EBITDA multiplier, but the company is seeing strong, double-digit earnings growth in its core business (natural gas), is adding new assets to its pipeline network, and has rock-solid dividend coverage: according to its capital framework, Kinder Morgan plans with distributable cash flow of $2.34/share in FY 2025, meaning the midstream enterprise calculates with a dividend coverage ratio of 2.0X. For comparison, Enterprise Products Partners, which is by far the most reliable and consistent payer in the midstream industry, had a distribution coverage ratio of 1.6X.

Risks with Kinder Morgan

The biggest risk for Kinder Morgan relates to a potential slowdown in U.S. natural gas demand as well as a deteriorating dividend coverage profile. Energy demand projections are supported by a growing utilization of U.S. data centers, so I consider it highly unlikely that we are going to see a deceleration of growth in the U.S. midstream energy sector. What would get me to change my mind about Kinder Morgan is if the enterprise scaled back growth investments/CapEx in the natural gas business, which is seeing the strongest earnings growth in the portfolio.

Final thoughts

Kinder Morgan is a rapidly growing natural gas champion in the U.S. midstream energy industry that is managing to add new growth projects to its capital budget. Natural gas-related growth projects are key to the expansion of the firm's project backlog, which in turn is going to benefit from the growth of the Data Center industry. Further, Kinder Morgan expects 2.0X dividend coverage in FY 2025, and with key measures like natural gas EBDA growing 10% Y/Y in the second quarter, I believe Kinder Morgan will continue to prioritize growth in this specific segment.

While Kinder Morgan's shares are slightly more expensive than the average midstream platform, I like that Kinder Morgan is adding new assets/pipelines to its portfolio and pushing its natural gas development business, all of which are going to make incremental DCF contributions going forward.

Manager Commentary

As of 06/30/2025

“At a granular level, we believe geopolitical tensions and tariffs favor smaller, more localized companies with local supply chains.”

Cedar Street Asset Management

Market in Review

Financial markets experienced varying degrees of volatility during the second quarter of 2025, as governments and economic policymakers adjusted to new priorities and trade policies of the new Trump administration, while de-emphasizing geopolitical tensions. Global equities, as measured by the MSCI World Index, dropped by more than 10% following Trump’s tariff-oriented “Liberation Day” announcements, but sharply rebounded to finish the quarter with a gain of 11.6%. Yields on the U.S. 10-year Treasury finished at 4.23%, not far from where they started the quarter at 4.21%, despite a 60-basis-point, intra-quarter move. Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar declined by about 7% for a total decline of 10.7% for the year.

In response to heightened volatility and lower inflationary pressures, most developed market central banks opted to lower monetary policy rates even as the U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) maintained its rate at a level that is among the highest in the developed world. For most non-U.S. developed markets, the combination of lower rates and currency appreciation meant that growth-style equities largely outperformed value-style equities and small-cap companies generally outperformed large-cap companies. In our view, when interest rates decline, investors tend to prefer growth equities, and when currencies appreciate, exporters (which tend to be large-cap companies) tend to underperform their small-cap peers.

In non-U.S. developed markets, the combination of currency appreciation, declining interest rates, and outperformance of both small-cap and large-cap equities relative to U.S. equities, suggests a continuation of the preference for non-U.S. financial assets that began in the first quarter. While elements of heightened speculative fervor may still exist, especially within AI-themed investments, in the U.S., we believe the demand for non-U.S. financial assets is gaining momentum.

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund’s current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund’s total annual operating expenses. The net expense ratios for this fund are subject to a contractual management fee waiver and/or expense limitation agreement, excluding interest expense and acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), through 02/28/2026.

Portfolio Performance

During the second quarter, the Harbor International Small Cap Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned 14.64%, underperforming its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index, which returned 16.59%.

We believe the primary factors driving underperformance were interest rate cuts by most developed market central banks and fund flows into passive investments. Historically, lower interest rates have tended to favor growth investments over value. Meanwhile, passive fund flows tend to favor larger-cap companies, even those that nominally fall within our small-cap benchmark. We note, as of June 30, 2025, companies with a market cap of more than $5 billion accounted for 29% of the benchmark, versus just 14% on June 30, 2024. For the benchmark, we estimate that the $5 billion-plus market-cap cohort generated a 26.5% return during the quarter, versus an 11.7% return for companies with market caps below $2 billion.

From a sector perspective, stock selection in Information Technology and Industrials was the biggest contributor to performance. This was outweighed by negative stock selection in Materials, Financials, and Health Care, which were the biggest detractors during the quarter.

From a geographic perspective, stock selection in Italy and Belgium and allocation effects in Ireland were the biggest contributors to performance, however, stock selection in Switzerland and Australia and a lack of allocation to Israel were the biggest detractors.

Contributors and Detractors

There were several detractors to performance during the quarter. Ontex, a Belgium private label maker of diapers, adult incontinence, and feminine-care products, experienced weaker profitability due to unexpected cost increases and restructuring expenses. Nabtesco, a Japanese factory automation and hydraulic equipment maker, continued to experience weakness in its end-markets, especially China. Nakanishi, a leading global manufacturer of dental drills, experienced slowing orders due to tariff concerns. Husqvarna, a Swedish maker of robotic mowers and gardening equipment, is facing accelerating competition for new end-markets. Victrex, the largest manufacturer of high-end PEEK polymer, experienced weaker earnings due to tariff concerns and a weaker U.S. dollar.

There were also several top contributors. Spectris, a U.K.-listed maker of scientific test and measurement equipment, received a buyout offer from private equity. X-FAB Silicon Foundries, an analog semiconductor foundry, reported earnings and an outlook that exceeded expectations after several disappointing quarters. Tinexta, an IT services and digital-identity services provider for businesses, mostly in Italy, reported better-than-expected earnings. Anritsu, a Japanese maker of scientific test and measurement equipment is seeing end-markets rebound after a sustained downturn, and Serco Group, an outsourced facilities management and training services provider, continues to win military contracts in the U.S. and U.K.

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we initiated eight new positions, as heightened volatility increased our overall opportunity set. One new position, AEON Financial Service Company (“AFS”), is the financial services arm of the Japanese retail giant, AEON. While most of AFS is geared toward Japanese consumers, the company’s Thai, Malaysian, Vietnamese, and Hong Kong subsidiaries capture consumer growth opportunities in some of the fastest-growing Asian economies. AFS benefits from cheap funding through AEON Bank deposits, which are recycled into consumer revolving loans, cash advance loans, and mortgages, while AFS continues to take market share in credit cards. Credit cards only have 43% penetration in Japan versus 80% in the U.S. Trading at about 60% of book value, we believe there are multiple potential avenues of value creation for this stock.

We also initiated a position in Spectris, a British test and measurement equipment provider. A mix of macro factors caused shares to reflect multiyear-low valuations, in our view. But with business activity showing signs of improvement, we believe the company could see a return to growth in the coming quarters. (After our entry, KKR and Advent both indicated a desire to acquire the business at a significant premium, which will likely result in a short but very favorable holding period for the portfolio.)

During the quarter we exited five positions, including ESR Group. ESR operates a real estate fund management business, primarily on e-commerce logistics and data centers. After receiving a buyout offer from a private equity consortium, we exited our position rather than risk a deal failure — a very real possibility in the current geopolitical environment. We also exited our position in Swedish gardening equipment firm Husqvarna, which we believe is likely to continue to struggle with its two-front war battling the increasing intensity of Chinese competitors and U.S. tariffs.

Sector Underweights and Overweights

During the quarter, Consumer Staples replaced Industrials as the second-largest overweight, and Consumer Discretionary replaced Financials as the second-largest underweight. As always, the portfolio’s sector allocations are a function of bottom-up stock selection consistent with our value-oriented philosophy and reflect specific idiosyncratic opportunities available to us rather than any active sector bet.

Outlook

Despite a sharp second quarter rebound, forward earnings multiples for the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index, our proxy for non-U.S. developed market small caps, ended the quarter at a forward price-to-earnings (“P/E”) multiple of 15x, below the 10-year average of 18x. Other metrics such as price-to-book (“P/B”) and enterprise value divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (“EV/EBITDA”) also indicate valuation levels that are at or below historical averages, while the second quarter dividend yield, at 3.3%, is above the 2.7% average. The MSCI EAFE Index indicates valuation levels that are elevated relative to the 10-year average on forward P/E, P/B, and EV/EBITDA, with dividend yields below average. Hence, relative to large-cap peers and their own history, non-U.S. developed market small caps still appear to be attractively priced, in our view.

The macroeconomic backdrop also continues to favor smaller foreign companies relative to larger ones. Relative to the U.S. dollar, most developed market currencies are strengthening despite central bank policies that are lowering monetary policy rates. We believe currencies were driven more by capital flows than interest rates in the second quarter and expect this dynamic to continue for the foreseeable future, as capital repatriation continues to drive asset flows out of the U.S. Stronger currencies tend to favor smaller-cap companies because they are less export-oriented. Meanwhile, lower interest rates, as most developed market central banks (except the Fed) loosen monetary policy, tend to disproportionately favor smaller companies because they tend to have higher levels of leverage.

At a granular level, we believe geopolitical tensions and tariffs favor smaller, more localized companies with local supply chains. Even the export-oriented companies in the portfolio that were impacted by previous trade tensions in 2017 have reconfigured their supply chains to limit tariff impacts.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Fund performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Past performance reflects the beneficial effect of any expense waivers or reimbursements, without which returns would have been lower. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

Important Information

Risks

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. Investing in international and emerging markets poses special risks, including potentially greater price volatility due to social, political and economic factors, as well as currency exchange rate fluctuations. These risks are more severe for securities of issuers in emerging market regions. Stocks of small cap companies pose special risks, including possible illiquidity and greater price volatility than stocks of larger, more established companies.

Benchmarks

The MSCI EAFE Small Cap (ND) Index is an equity index which captures small cap representation across developed market countries around the world, excluding the U.S. and Canada. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment. Benchmark returns are adjusted for withholding taxes.

Disclosures

All holdings-related data is provided by FactSet. Because FactSet relies on external sources for its data, that data may differ slightly from actual values maintained by Harbor Funds.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Beta is a rolling three year, unless the Fund has a track record of less than three years, in which case it is a rolling one year. Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments. Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Distributed by Harbor Funds Distributors, Inc.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period. Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

data.title: Virgin Galactic: If Milestones Are Met, The Worst Is Mostly Priced In
data.description:
Since I wrote my previous article on Virgin Galactic (NYSE:SPCE) on April 30, adopting a skeptical stance toward the company, interestingly, shares of the Tustin, California-based company have risen in value by about 24%.

As I pointed out, SPCE's thesis is quite straightforward: either the company takes off or it crashes (no pun intended).

And the market has basically priced SPCE in line with the second alternative.

Despite occasional short-term respites, in this most recent case caused by hopeful statements from the company's management team regarding the milestones of its Delta Class spaceship, SPCE's trajectory over the last six months has been one of declines (10.7%), totaling declines of (38.7%) year-to-date.

This bearish momentum appears to be an outline of the unsustainability of future narratives (optionalities) reversing such a rate of cash burn, and especially the latent need for shareholder dilution.

After all, who in their right mind would agree to buy a business knowing that it will be diluted for at least the next two years, with no guarantee of turning cash flow positive until then?

For this reason, I am fundamentally skeptical of Virgin Galactic's thesis, even though I acknowledge that its business sounds inviable and disruptive, and the management team has arguably worked hard to keep milestones evolving within a plausible timeline considering the company's liquidity.

To track the thesis of a speculative pre-revenue company like Virgin Galactic, I believe that using a framework where it is possible to mix timeline milestones + cash burn + dilution math can make the most sense.

And in order to update my thesis, in this article, I will look for a bull and bear scenario for what is currently being estimated.

Virgin Galactic Flights Are Coming Eventually

Since there are barely any operations, SPCE's main driver is the progress of its spacecraft development timeline.

In July 2021, there was the first flight of Unity-22, with a full crew (4 mission specialists), including Richard Branson. From then on, Unity flew every several months, with long periods of maintenance and preparation.

But from the outset, it was expected that each Unity flight would be more of a technological and marketing demonstration than a profitable business. After all, the VSS Unity spacecraft could only carry 4 passengers + 2 pilots, with each flight costing millions for a revenue of only ~$450k to $600k per flight, given the ticket of around $250k-450k per passenger.

So Unity's mission was much more about validating technology and customer experience and transitioning to Delta Class, which would be Virgin Galactic's business case.

A few years passed, and it wasn't until July last year that the Delta Class facility in Phoenix/Arizona, was completed. And in March of this year, the assembly of the first Delta Class ship in Phoenix finally began.

The most recent results reported for the second quarter of 2025 gave details of the construction, with wing assembly and fuselage expected by the end of 4Q25 and the beginning of 1Q26, but with the goal of commercial flights remaining in 2026. The company's plan is to resume private astronaut flights in the Fall of 2026 (recently delayed in August vs. an expected Summer of 2026).

Of course, Virgin Galactic has a history of promising very ambitious milestones and then delaying them for technical, operational, or supply chain reasons. That's why, arguably, setting "on time" execution expectations tends to be too optimistic.

Liquidity & Lift Off

Virgin Galactic currently has $469.3 million in total cash & short-term investments on its balance sheet (or $508 million if you consider marketable securities), which, by the way, is basically net cash, given only $6.1 million in net debt.

In the last twelve months, SPCE reported a negative OCF of $291.7 million, which has been an average of $72.9 million in quarterly cash burn. What has preserved the company's liquidity has been the $38 million average equity issuance raised per quarter, for the last four consecutive quarters, at the expense of diluting its shareholders. In fact, this marks the impressive sequential increase in outstanding shares of 42% YoY in 2023; 77.6% YoY in 2024, and finally 102.2% YoY in the last twelve months.

Therefore, it's more than certain that more dilution will be coming very soon, in my view.

Virgin Galactic reported free cash flow figures for the three quarters of this year of negative $116 million in 4Q24, further negative $121 million in 1Q25, and then negative $114 million in 2Q25.

In addition, the company has guidance of negative $100 million to negative $110 million for 3Q25. In other words, this gives a cash runway of just four quarters (or thirteen months) based on what the company can operate with its current cash.

Virgin Galactic seeks to sell the idea that the capital on hand, using the ATM equity offering program, is calculated and under control. Within its LV-X program (expansion of the Delta Class fleet), the company estimates that its initial fleet of two spaceships can support 125 flights per year, and bring in $450 million in revenue, and from that be converted into $100 million in adjusted EBITDA. And with Delta #3 and #4, the trend is $1 billion in revenues and EBITDA multiplied by 4x—going from a 20%-25% margin to 45%-50%—a calculation made from 275 annual flights manned by an average of 1,650 passengers (considering an average ticket of $600,000 per astronaut).

As the ramp-up of Delta Class commercial flights is expected to be from mid-2026, analysts have placed a dose of skepticism on these targets, expecting $338.4 million of revenues to be generated only in 2027, and the $1 billion the company promises with the expanded fleet only in 2029.

Base, Bull, and Bear Outlooks

Of course, for estimates to be valid, Virgin Galactic needs to deliver flawless execution, which historically has not been the case.

But relying on the market consensus and what Virgin Galactic has guided, we can estimate some plausible scenarios by separating them into base, bull, and bear cases.

And here are my assumptions:

Since SPCE is a very high-risk thesis, and using an FCF multiple range of 6- 12x for early-stage commercial space, we can estimate the following scenarios:

  • FCF Base case scenario of $112.5 million at a multiple of 8x, implying an equity value of ~$900 million.
  • FCF bull scenario at $225 million at a multiple of 10x, implying an equity value of ~$2.25 billion.
  • FCF bear scenario at $26 million at a multiple of 6x, implying an equity value of ~$156 million.

Although this is still a speculative exercise, it at least gives an idea of what the market tends to price in based on current estimates and, in more extreme cases, how much downside the thesis could still have.

The Bottom Line

Virgin Galactic seems to be relatively on track with its Delta Class launch schedule, and this is undoubtedly a very positive point for a pre-profit cash burn company in a dramatic race against time.

Because of its constant need for funding through ATM equity offering programs, investors who are in it for the long term need to be aware that they will likely be massively diluted ahead, at least for the next two years, and that the milestones' progress must be significant in order to offset further stock price downward pressure.

I prefer to stay away from such speculative theses from companies with such a tight balance sheet, and capital being deployed into a yet-to-be-explored market—although I realize that, in Virgin Galactic's specific case, at current prices, a plausible bear case scenario may already be partially embedded.

Since I don't see enough evidence to support a minimally healthy risk-return, I prefer not to touch SPCE with a ten-foot pole and would suggest others exercise caution as well.

data.title: Anfield Energy: A Solid Choice To Speculate On The Trump Administration's Energy Independence Goals
data.description:
Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) approximately doubled in price on Wednesday after a report that the Trump Administration is seeking an equity stake in the company. While this news has had a positive impact on several lithium juniors with interests in the United States, I believe that the key takeaway here is that there is an aggressive personal and political interest in the Unleashing American Energy executive order produced by the President on the first day of his second term. Uranium stocks have been a benefactor of increased speculation produced by the executive order, with Energy Fuels Inc. (UUUU) and Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC) up well over 100% this year.

Anfield Energy Inc. (NASDAQ:AEC) is a recent NASDAQ listing that is a smaller peer to UUUU and UEC. It has so far not generated a lot of market attention, except from UEC itself. UEC owns a 32.4% stake in Anfield, having doubled its position in the company in June. AEC owns the Shootaring Canyon Mill, one of three licensed conventional mills in the United States. The other two are the Sweetwater Mill owned by UEC and the White Mesa Mill owned by UUUU. Because of that, I believe that these three companies would be the top targets should the Trump administration pursue an equity stake in domestic uranium supply in a similar way it has been rumored to be doing for lithium.

ListingTrack on X has compiled a summary of publicly traded uranium companies by market cap. AEC is the smallest of the twelve companies mentioned and is of considerably lower valuation than UEC and UUUU. If one of these three companies became a buyout target, there would be a massive positive impact on the other two. However, I believe that AEC as the smallest player would have the largest percentage impact. Therefore I am betting on that one to be one of the biggest winners in the uranium bull market and suggest that other uranium bulls consider it as well.

In addition to its high beta potential as small cap play, AEC has lagged its two larger industry peers over the last year, but especially since July. Part of this could be that it just recently listed on the NASDAQ and prior to that was trading on the OTC as a penny stock. With the uplisting and a reverse split, AEC can now be purchased by institutional investors that have already bought UEC and UUUU. Some may consider buying UEC as an effective purchase of AEC due to its significant stake in the company, while others may consider UEC's purchase of AEC as a stamp of approval and buy the individual stock as well.

While my bullish thesis on AEC is primarily focused as an under-the-radar uranium play with a low market cap, it has gotten on the radar of the Federal Government on its individual merits. AEC's Velvet-Wood project in Utah was the first uranium project to be approved by the U.S. government under the fast-track permitting of energy and mining projects. Anfield acquired this project alongside the Shootaring Canyon Mill and it lies approximately 180 miles away from the mill. AEC's Slick Rock project on the Colorado side of the border sits 200 miles away, as illustrated in AEC's Preliminary Economic Assessment.

Risk factors to buying AEC

Being a high beta small cap stock in a hot sector works both ways. In a uranium bull market, AEC should have outsized gains. In a bear market, it's at risk of underperforming larger and more financially stable peers. AEC's balance sheet (in Canadian Dollars) as of June 30, 2025 shows $11 million in cash and working capital. The burn rate is around $3 million a quarter, with about half of that being exploration expenses which can be discretionary in times of low cash resources. So the company has about 9-12 months of cash runway given current spending levels with the possibility to stretch that as far out as two years during tough times.

Given the environment for uranium and UEC's obvious interest in the company, I don't expect raising cash to be an issue. I expect continued raises and a likely increase in drilling expenses that these investments will allow. So the primary risk here is dilution rather than insolvency risk. According to the PEA, there will be significant capital costs associated with Velvet-Wood and Slick Rock. $112 million in capex will be needed over two years of development and an additional $6 million in working capital needed before AEC earns a penny in revenue. These costs are comparable to the current market cap, so investors need to prepare for as much as 100% dilution should AEC decide to develop the mine on its own.

The pre-tax NPV of the project is $238 million assuming an 8% discount rate. However, that is based on a uranium price of $70 and vanadium price of $12 per pound, respectively. There is a reasonable cushion on the downside, with the project being NPV breakeven at $42 per pound for uranium and $7.20 per pound vanadium. Uranium spent several years well below that price prior to 2022, but I don't foresee that happening any time soon again given current economic conditions. However, it remains a risk to getting the project started.

The company provided a sensitivity chart to commodity prices in the PEA. Notable since the spot uranium price is currently $82. At $77 per pound (and $13.20 per pound vanadium), the pre-tax NPV of the project rises to $300 million.

Given the substantial capex but also the profitable nature of the project at current commodity prices, I expect that Anfield will likely seek a senior partner to build out Velvet-Wood, with UEC being the most obvious candidate. A partnership model significantly improves AEC's risk profile and floor, but also caps its upside as revenue will be shared with that senior partner.

AEC at this early stage is fraught with risks and uncertainties, but I believe that the valuation is low enough to account for them. Risks and uncertainties also represent near and mid-term positive catalysts should AEC successfully navigate through them.

Conclusion: I am very bullish on AEC's stock and warrants

In addition to its NASDAQ listing, AEC trades on the TSX under the same symbol and also has publicly traded warrants under the symbol AEC.WT. The warrants have a strike price of $13.50 Canadian, expire in May 2027 and require 75 warrants to exercise for one share. I appreciate the ability to buy warrants on a speculative opportunity such as Anfield because it requires me to risk far less capital while having an aggressive upside. The risk to buying warrants is if the stock stays relatively flat for the next two years, just under the $13.50 strike price. I don't believe that will be the case. Anfield will be a boom or bust type of stock that either nets investors well over 100% from this point on or becomes a loser in excess of 50%. The upside potential is captured with the warrants while the total dollar value of an investment loss is capped in the downside scenario even if the percentage loss on the warrants ends up being 100%.

U.S. uranium listings with domestic projects have been extremely strong performers during the Trump Administration and I don't see that changing even with a regime change in the future. Energy security, independence and dealing with climate change all run through nuclear power and for the most part it has bi-partisan support. Anfield has been a relative laggard and under-the-radar of the investment community. But it has been on the radar of the U.S. Federal Government and UEC. For that reason I am very bullish on AEC, even at this pre-revenue stage.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EWZ: Valuation Is Reasonable, But Business Quality Remains A Concern
data.description:
The S&P 500 and NASDAQ are rising from one all-time high to the next, and valuation levels are reaching historical extremes. Therefore, it might be wise to diversify globally going forward.

Emerging markets have disappointed over the last decade. This has led to attractive valuations, which could lead to outperformance over the next decade. One of the biggest mistakes I see the majority of investors commit is extrapolating past performance into the future.

According to this source, Brazil is one of the most attractively valued markets globally. Today, we're going to dive deeper into the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (NYSEARCA:EWZ) to see if it offers value in an exuberant market environment.

iShares MSCI Brazil ETF

EWZ seeks exposure to large and mid-sized companies in Brazil. The net expense ratio is 0.59%, which is on the high side for an ETF, but in line with peers that provide a single-country view.

The turnover rate is 22%, which seems a little too high. It leads to unnecessary transaction fees that drag on performance. Combined with the relatively high expense ratio, and a decent amount of performance is eaten up by fees.

P/E Ratio

One of the strongest selling points for Brazilian equities is the relatively low valuations. Yahoo Finance claims EWZ's average P/E ratio is 10.62, while the Wall Street Journal (WSJ) puts it at 13.91.

Having analyzed dozens of ETFs already, the WSJ numbers seem to be more accurate for the earnings multiple on a TTM basis. But to make sure, we're going to calculate the average P/E for the top holdings a little later on.

In both cases, the valuations look relatively attractive, as long as the underlying businesses are of quality, which we're going to find out down below.

Dividend Yield

A low valuation usually leads to high dividend yields, and EWZ is no exception. In 2024, EWZ distributed $2.01, and the current share price is $31.32 for a dividend yield of 6.4%.

In the first half of 2025, however, the distributions were cut by 52.82%, which will likely lead to a lower forward dividend yield. If we assume EWZ will distribute $1.60 this year, the yield drops to 5.11%.

A 5% to 6% dividend yield looks very attractive in the current environment, but just by looking at EWZ distributions over the last decades, it becomes clear that the underlying businesses are cyclical. In other words, we can't rely on the dividends.

Cyclical businesses tend to be poor long-term performers, unless bought at the low points during severe bear markets. EWZ distributions have risen from $0.64 in 2001 (its first full year after inception) to $2.81 in 2010, only to fall back to $0.60 in the 6 years thereafter.

Since then, they have been on a steady rise to an all-time high of $3.52 set in 2022, and over the last few years have declined rapidly. Not surprisingly, the distributions tend to rise and fall with the commodity cycles, as Brazil's economy tends to be commodity-focused.

Sector Allocation

The largest sector is financials, with 37.55% of total assets invested, followed by energy, utilities, basic materials and industrials.

The sector allocation isn't unusual for an emerging market, but it focuses on low-growth and cyclical industries, which likely won't lead to outsized long-term total returns, unless bought at very low multiples.

Top 10 Holdings

EWZ only holds 59 stocks, which isn't unusual for a smaller single country ETF. The top 10 holdings represent 57.10% of total assets. It's pretty concentrated and shouldn't be a core holding in a well-diversified portfolio.

Nu Holdings leads the list with a 12.34% weight, followed by Itau Unibanco, Vale, and Petrobras.

Top Holdings: Valuations, Growth Rates & Quality

When it comes to determining future expected returns, there are three important metrics: the starting valuation, the expected growth rates, and the business quality.

Ideally, we want to buy high-quality companies with high growth rates at rock-bottom valuations. While that sounds too good to be true, if we're patient and turn enough rocks, those kinds of companies can be found. But in this exuberant market environment, where bargains aren't abundant, it leads to a concentrated portfolio.

If we buy a broader ETF (in a world of high valuations), we have to either pay more for our stocks or cut back on our quality or growth expectations. To determine if EWZ offers an attractive risk/reward profile at current valuations, let's dive deeper into the companies. And we're going to start with the average P/E ratio and the past growth rates over the commodity cycles.

Company: P/E Ratio Annual EPS Growth Rate (Past 10 Years):
Nu Holdings 34.36 Nil
Itau Unibanco 8.63 0%
Vale 8.48 Nil
Petrobras 6.18 Nil
Petrobras 6.18 Nil
Bank Bradesco 8.18 Nil
B3 SA 14.90 7.89%
Companhia De Saneamento 9.34 33.42%
Centrais Electricas Brasileiras 18.70 Nil
Weg 30.87 20.26%

(Gurufocus)

Just by looking at the cash flow statements over the last 10 years, it becomes clear that EWZ mostly holds what I perceive to be below-average businesses.

Most of the companies generate erratic cash flows, deeply negative at times. Over the cycles, there's not much growth, if any. It's not ideal for long-term investment.

Investors in EWZ need to hope for a prolonged and sustained commodity price increase to generate outsized returns, which in my view, makes the ETF speculative. Unless it's trading at rock-bottom valuations (and currently it's not), it won't make my personal portfolio.

That said, the average P/E ratio of the largest companies is 14.58, which is roughly in line with the numbers provided by the WSJ. Given the quality of the underlying businesses, the valuation doesn't impress me, but it's low enough to not rate it a "sell" but a "hold".

EWZ Performance Over 25 Years

EWZ has been around for 25 years; that's a long enough time to judge its performance. Over a quarter of a century, the ETF has gained 321% on a total return basis. That's a 5.92% CAGR, dividends included.

If we just look at the share price performance, results have been even less impressive.

The share price gained 66% since inception, which is a 2.05% CAGR and reflects the below-average quality of the underlying businesses and the sluggish growth over the cycles. As long as we don't enter a major commodity bull, an ETF like EWZ will likely keep disappointing.

Comparison Against Other ETFs

Over the last few years, EWZ has outperformed the Global X Brazil Active ETF (BRAZ), but underperformed the Franklin FTSE Brazil ETF (FLBR).

If we judge EWZ's and FLBR's performance over a longer time horizon, however, it's almost identical. Only the VanEck Brazil Small-Cap ETF (BRF) keeps underperforming.

Investing in small-caps, especially in emerging markets, may need an active approach.

Conclusion

On one hand, EWZ trades at a modest valuation, offers a compelling (but unreliable) dividend yield, and provides exposure to an emerging market with growth prospects. On the other hand, the quality of the underlying businesses is questionable, growth has been subdued and choppy at best, and the long-term performance isn't convincing.

As long as we don't see another major commodity bull, EWZ will likely keep underperforming, which makes the ETF more of a speculation than a compelling long-term investment. I rate it a "hold" at current valuations, which aren't excessive, but also not in bargain territory.

I believe other emerging markets like the Philippines offer higher quality at similar valuations, even though the dividend yield is less tempting.

data.title: Albemarle Corporation DEP SHS REPSTG A: Mandatory Converts, Not Traditional Preferred Shares
data.description:
Thesis

Many investors just look at naming conventions and yields and forget to do a full due diligence on what they buy. Retail investors need to understand that in finance a naming convention does not always translate to a familiar reality, and investors need to read the 'fine print'.

In today's article we are going to deal with the Albemarle Corporation DEP SHS REPSTG A (NYSE:ALB.PR.A) and highlight for readers what these securities actually are, what they offer in terms of risks and rewards, and why they were issued in the first place.

Who is Albemarle? A lithium producer

Let us start off with the parent company, namely Albemarle (NYSE:ALB):

Albemarle Corporation is an American specialty chemicals manufacturing company headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina. The company is a global leader in the production of lithium.

Lithium is a critical component of batteries, including EV batteries. After a massive spike in prices in 2022, lithium pricing has come back to a more palatable historic trend:

As you would expect with any commodity producer, ALB saw its common shares skyrocket with lithium prices, only to come back down to earth in the past two years:

After spiking up more than 240%, the common shares are back to flat on a 5-year lookback. More importantly though, the company has been taking the correct measures to improve its balance sheet after chasing higher lithium prices with capex.

Why did the company issue mandatory convertibles? Balance sheet optimization

Let us start by looking at the capital structure of the company:

As per Seeking Alpha the company has $3.7 billion in total debt and $9.4 billion in market cap. The net debt figure is lower because ALB has a very large cash balance.

When a company sees its revenues decrease substantially and its net income go negative, it is going to be hard pressed to raise debt because it will signal future deterioration in figures:

Please note how revenues decreased from $7 bil in 2022 to $4.9 on a TTM basis, while operating income moved from positive $2.5 bil to negative -183 mm on a TTM basis.

Given the shifting dynamics in the operating environment, the company chose to raise capital via mandatory convertible preferred shares:

March 6, 2024: Holders of the Depositary Shares will be entitled to a proportional fractional interest in the rights and preferences of the Preferred Stock, including conversion, dividend, liquidation and voting rights, subject to the provisions of a deposit agreement. Unless earlier converted, each share of the Preferred Stock will automatically convert on or around March 1, 2027 into between 7.6180 and 9.1400 shares of common stock of the Company, par value $0.01 per share ("Common Stock") (and, correspondingly, each Depositary Share will automatically convert into between 0.3809 and 0.4570 shares of Common Stock), subject to customary anti-dilution adjustments, determined based on the volume-weighted average price of the Common Stock over the 20 consecutive trading day period beginning on, and including, the 21st scheduled trading day prior to March 1, 2027.

This structure ensured the new securities did not affect debt ratios (they are listed in the equity line) and that the company did not bomb its common shareholders at the bottom. Issuing mandatory converts basically signals that the company is expecting a bottoming in share price in the near future, and thus does not want to raise common equity capital at depressed prices. Furthermore as we will see from the conversion arithmetic, the market has worked against the converts holders, which suffered losses as the common stock moved lower from the $131/share in March 2024.

Not your usual preferred shares

Investors are used to preferred shares as fixed income instruments that offer a high yield and are usually perpetual and sometimes cumulative. In essence traditional preferred shares are a fixed income instrument. ALB.PR.A is an equity instrument because there will be a mandatory conversion in March 2027, and an investor gets a certain compensation until that point (rate of 7.25%).

Make no mistake about it - via ALB.PR.A an investor is taking a view on the common shares and on lithium prices in essence. Because the company considered common shares to be underpriced in March 2024, it chose this mandatory converts preferred shares structure. Do not buy ALB.PR.A for the dividend, buy it only if you have a view on lithium prices and the common shares.

The conversion mechanics can be found in the prospectus and the quarterly report, but they boil down to the following:

  • If the common share price is below $109.41, you will get 0.457 common shares, which will be less than the $50 liquidation preference (109.41x0.457=50)
  • If the common share price is between $109.41 and $131.27 you will get common shares equal to $50/(ALB stock price).
  • If the common share price is above $131.27 you will get 0.3809 common shares.

As it stands the converts will result in common shares with a lower value, thus the depressed price of $38.77 versus issuance date (just multiply the common stock price now with 0.457 to get a rough estimate for converts when prices are below $109/share).

We do not believe we are going to have a 'V' shaped recovery in lithium prices, and even analysts agree with that view:

The average Wall St price target is $86.65 /share for the commons, with EPS going positive only in 2026:

Most of the uncertainty regarding earnings comes from geopolitical risk factors rather than company specific. ALB has cut costs and done some savvy moves with its balance sheet, all while maintaining an investment grade rating:

The above screen is the bond ladder for Albemarle, and we can see its senior unsecured debt trades very well, with the 2044 bonds yielding only 6.3% and having a BBB- rating. The market is not concerned about the solvency of the company, but equity investors (and converts investors) should be concerned with profitability.

Latest developments and risk factors

We mentioned geo-political factors before, and they should be in the center of investors' minds. China has subsidized in many instances industries which it considers critical, and any decisions to open or close mines from the jurisdiction have massive ripple effects:

Albemarle (NYSE:ALB) closed -11.5% in Tuesday's trading, leading a raft of sharp losses in the lithium sector, after executives at Chinese battery giant Contemporary Amperex Technology reportedly told employees to prepare to resume production at the Jianxiawo lithium mine and recall front-line workers, according to Bloomberg.

Kindly note the above action comes after the company was forced by the central government in August 2025 to shut production due to an expiring permit:

The halt at the Jianxiawo lepidolite mine in Jiangxi province’s Yichun city, a hub for China’s lithium production, came after the mine’s permit expired on August 9. CATL confirmed the closure on Monday (August 11), saying it is seeking a permit extension but offering no timeline for resuming output. The shutdown will last at least three months, according to people familiar with the matter cited by Bloomberg.

Make no mistake about it - Chinese producers are tightly controlled by the central government via permitting, and they represent just actors in a larger geo-political game.

In the U.S. the government is also helping with local production via loans, but the current administration is keen to also obtain an equity stake:

Lithium Americas stock doubled Wednesday as the Trump administration is seeking an equity stake in the mining company, which is based in Vancouver, British Columbia. The White House proposed the equity stake as Lithium Americas renegotiates the terms of a $2.2 billion loan from the Department of Energy for its Thacker Pass mine in Nevada, a Trump administration official told CNBC. Reuters first reported the equity stake proposal.

While from a technical standpoint lithium prices seem to have bottomed, they are nonetheless very much subject to political jousting.

Conclusion

The Series A Mandatory Convertible Preferred Shares are securities from the lithium producer ALB. Unlike traditional preferred shares, ALB.PR.A will be forced to convert into common shares in March 2027, thus representing a take on common equity. Despite the 7.25% coupon paid until then, prospective investors should look at the risk and rewards associated with the common shares, and understand the various geo-political factors at play given the massive influence on prices from Chinese producers.

data.title: Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by extremely high volatility. April started with President Trump’s “Liberation Day” on which he announcement of broad-based global tariffs. These levies were far higher than anticipated, leading equity markets to pull back dramatically. Over the course of the quarter, the administration began negotiating with other countries to moderate some tariffs and pause others for 90 days; headlines about this process led to swings in assorted global markets. Slowly but surely, most markets began to recover. The S&P 500® Index, for example, hit a nadir in early April but rebounded to a new record high by the end of June.

There was some dispersion within the U.S. as larger multi-national stocks (represented by the S&P 500 and Nasdaq) outperformed domestic small/mid-cap stocks and industrials (represented by the Russell 2000® Index, Dow Jones Industrial Average, and S&P Mid-Cap 400® Index). With inflation remaining close to target rates, and apprehension about how trade-related issues would impact growth, many central banks lowered interest rates. However, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) refrained from lowering rates, taking a more cautious approach, while acknowledging that tariffs may drive future inflation. This created tension with the Trump administration, which continued to pressure the Fed to lower rates.

The U.S. dollar weakened dramatically this year partly out of fear that tariffs may have a negative impact on growth. This effect on global growth has also depressed some commodity prices, particularly within industrial inputs like base metals and energies. In addition to the economic uncertainty, markets have also been rocked by global conflicts, both ongoing (Russia/Ukraine, Israel/Gaza) and new (Israel/Iran). The U.S. involvement in strikes on Iran’s nuclear capabilities initially led to a spike in oil prices, which retreated after it appeared there would be no further hostilities.

Performance

The Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Class I (MUTF:ASFYX) shares returned -11.11% during the second quarter of 2025, while the Fund’s benchmark, the Credit Suisse Managed Futures Liquid Index, returned -12.08% for the quarter. It is important to note that there are material differences between the Fund and its benchmark. Another index that may be used for performance analysis is the SG Trend Index as it reflects a peer group of diversified, primarily trend-following investment managers. This benchmark returned -5.59% for the second quarter of 2025.

The Fund maintained broad diversification across trend horizons and approaches to trend following in the second quarter. Over the course of the quarter, the Fund’s losses came primarily from equities, with additional losses from fixed income and commodities. Currencies provided some small gains.

As equity markets gyrated over the quarter, the Fund’s long positions led to losses, particularly from international developed market equities. The largest losses came from the Dow Jones EURO STOXX 50 (Europe), the MSCI EAFE Index, and FTSE 100 Index (UK). Emerging market equities and U.S. equities also detracted, although the Fund had some small gains from short positions in the Russell 2000 Index.

In fixed income, losses were driven by long positions in U.S. fixed income, which detracted across the board; most international developed market bonds also detracted. The Fund had small gains from Italian bonds, while short-term rates also detracted from performance.

Commodities were more mixed, as gains from long positions in precious metals, especially gold, were outweighed by losses from base metals, energies, and agricultural commodities. Base metal losses came primarily from long positions in copper, while energy losses were driven by natural gas and Brent crude oil. In agricultural commodities, losses from long positions in coffee outweighed small gains from sugar, soymeal, and cocoa.

Currencies were also mixed, as gains from long positions in foreign currencies (short USD) slightly outweighed losses from short positions in foreign currencies (long USD). The largest gains came from the British pound, euro, and Polish zloty, while the largest losses came from the Canadian dollar, New Zealand dollar, and South African rand.

At the model level, all three approaches detracted from performance, with the adaptive models underperforming the short-horizon and basic multi-trend approaches.

While higher short-term rates are continuing to positively impact Fund performance, markets expectations are increasing for lower rates in the second half of 2025.

Risk Management

The portfolio is managed to an annualized volatility target of 17% or less.1 We scale portfolio positions as often as daily in an effort to keep total portfolio risk within its target range. As market volatility increases, position sizes are reduced, and as market volatility decreases, position sizes are increased. The Fund’s realized volatility during the quarter was 15.2%, higher than the long-term realized volatility for the Fund. We continue to scale the size of the Fund’s positions to keep total portfolio risk at or below its target.

Outlook

Clarity remains in short supply for most markets. Despite strengthening equity markets, concerns about the long-term effect of tariffs persist, especially when the administration’s pause expires. Another major question is whether the Fed will begin to cut rates, or whether it will maintain higher rates despite pressure from the administration and lower core inflation. It remains uncertain what impact the early-July tax bill will have on market sentiment going forward.

Finally, the expansion of the U.S. involvement in geopolitical conflicts may change the calculus of these ongoing struggles and further impact global markets. In general, the hope is for smoother sailing going into the second half of 2025 than we experienced during the first half.

1The Fund seeks to target a relatively stable level of annualized volatility (as measured by standard deviation), but the targeted volatility is subject to change. There is no guarantee that the target will be achieved and the realized volatility level of the Fund can be higher or lower than its target volatility at any given point in time. Volatility is not an indicator of expected return or a measure of protection against loss.

data.title: Weekly Economic Pulse: A Goldilocks Rate Cut
data.description:
The Federal Reserve cut their target for the federal funds rate by 25 basis points last week due to concerns about labor market weakness. Job creation has certainly been weak the last 3 or 4 months but that is largely due to the uncertainty created by the Trump administration’s haphazard implementation of their economic program. Well, not entirely. It is also due to the economic program itself, if you include immigration in that category and I certainly do. Contrary to the administration’s claims, immigration is a net positive for the economy and there is no evidence that immigrants take jobs from American citizens. There may be reasons to limit immigration but economic growth is not one of them. Growth is a function of workforce growth and productivity growth. The Trump economic bet has always been that they can get enough of the latter to offset their stifling of the former. Unfortunately, kicking immigrants out is quick and investing to raise productivity is not. So, the economy has slowed and the Fed, like everyone else, sees this in the economic data, especially the employment data.

The quandary facing the Fed is that we don’t know how much these policy changes will reduce growth and for how long. We also don’t know their impact on prices. Targeted tariffs, such as those used by Trump in his first term, cause relative price changes but the impact of universal tariffs is more difficult to predict, especially when combined with reduced immigration. If the Fed cuts rates due to concerns about growth they could actually create the inflation they fear. If lower immigration has reduced the economy’s capacity for growth – and it certainly has – and the Fed tries to maintain the pre-tariff rate of NGDP growth, it seems likely that the result will be higher inflation. Trying to maintain the same level of growth when the economy’s potential has been reduced would likely result in labor shortages, higher wages, and higher inflation.

Did the Fed do enough last week to avoid recession? Did they do too much so that we’ll get another burst of inflation? Based on the market reaction so far, it seems they got it just about right. Long-term nominal and real interest rates are up modestly since the cut, indicating a small rise in growth expectations. The 10-year Treasury yield is up about 12 basis points and the 10-year TIPS yield is up about the same; inflation expectations are unchanged since the cut. The economy has been growing a little below the long-term trend this year and the market seems to indicate that the Fed’s move will keep it that way.

Environment

The dollar and interest rates remain in short and intermediate-term downtrends but we may be setting up for a bounce in both. The dollar’s descent has stalled, essentially unchanged since mid-June. We made a new closing low last week but there was no follow through after the rate cut. Given current market positioning and sentiment, a bounce in the buck seems likely. We had an upside probe in late July but it failed quickly. Will this one face the same fate? I don’t know but with basically everyone negative on the dollar, a quick, strong rally seems more likely than not.

A rally in the dollar would probably coincide with a rise in US interest rates but I don’t think this is like last year’s September rate cut which pushed rates up 120 basis points over the last quarter of the year. The economy is facing more headwinds now and I think investors will need convincing before they bet on a return to trend or above trend growth.

Economy/Market Indicators

The spike in credit spreads we saw after the reciprocal tariff announcement in early April has been almost entirely erased. The low for this cycle was 2.59% so we’re just 10 basis points away. If the market gets worried about recession, widening spreads will likely be our first warning sign.

Economy/Economic Data

  • The Chicago Fed National Activity index improved from -0.28 to -0.12. The 3 month average improved from -0.20 to -0.18. All the sub-indexes (production & income, sales/orders/inventories, employment and personal consumption/housing) are right around the zero level indicating trend growth. We use the 3 month average to smooth out the month to month movements and a reading of -0.18 means growth slightly below trend. In fewer words – nothing has changed.
  • The Philly Fed manufacturing survey, unlike last week’s Empire State version, rose strongly. Why Philly is rising and NY isn’t I don’t know.
  • The Richmond Fed survey looked more like the NY version last week, dropping from -7 to -17
  • Jobless claims fell back to 231k after last week’s Texas weirdness. Companies may not be hiring much but they aren’t firing many either.
  • Housing starts and permits were down hard and improvement will only come with an improvement in sales; inventories are getting a little high.
  • The inflation data from Europe was generally pretty good, although UK is still quite high at 3.7% year over year. However, the rate of change is slowing and the MPC (UK equivalent of the FOMC) voted to hold rates steady.
